misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand why multiplying two numbers less than 1 gives an even smaller result.

visuals.ruleText: Multiplying by a number less than 1 makes things smaller

steps.narration: When a factor is less than one, multiplication scales down instead of up. Zero point four times zero point three means 'find forty percent of zero point three' — which is twelve percent of one, or zero point one two. The result is smaller than both factors.

visuals.label: 0.3 × 0.4 = shaded area of a 1×1 square (3 rows of 0.1 × 4 cols of 0.1)

steps.narration: Think of a square with side length one. Shade three tenths of the height and four tenths of the width. The shaded rectangle covers twelve hundredths of the full square — that is zero point one two. Area equals length times width, so zero point three times zero point four equals zero point one two.

steps.narname: Three tenths times four tenths: the digits multiply to give twelve, and tenths times tenths gives hundredths. Twelve hundredths is zero point one two — the decimal place count reflects the unit-size multiplication.

visuals.label: Tenths × tenths = hundredths — two decimal places in each factor → two in answer

steps.narration: Zero point one two. Tenths multiplied by tenths produce hundredths — that is why the answer has two decimal places when both factors have one each. The decimal-place count is a place-value consequence, not an arbitrary rule.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: expecting 0.3 × 0.4 to be larger than both factors. Multiplying by a value less than 1 scales down — 0.3 × 0.4 = 0.12, smaller than either factor.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiplying by something less than 1 makes the answer smaller. Zero point three of zero point four is twelve hundredths — smaller than both factors. Tenths times tenths give hundredths.
